Article.ArticleTitle: Strengthening nutrient resilience in rice (Oryza Sativa L.) under elevated CO2: insights into physiological, biochemical, and transcriptomic responses to biochar and CeO2 nanoparticles.

Abstract.AbstractText: The rise in atmospheric CO2 concentration is linked to a decline in the nutritional quality of staple crops, posing a significant challenge for human nutrition and food security. Mitigating the detrimental effects of elevated CO2 on crops is a significant issue. Cerium oxide nanoparticles and biochar have garnered attention in agriculture for their potential in enhancing crop productivity and quality. In this study, foliar applications of CeO2-NPs (0, 50, and 100 mg L-1) and biochar-amended soil were used to examine their combined regulatory effects on physiological, biochemical, and nutrient regulation mechanisms in rice grown under both ambient CO2 (400 μmol mol-1) and elevated CO2 (600 μmol mol-1) conditions. Elevated CO2 reduced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate, which negatively influence the nutrient regulation mechanism in rice plants. Notably, the BC. NPs. 100 treatment increased stomatal conductance and transpiration rate by 112.14 % and 148.09 %, respectively. BC. NPs combined treatment remarkably enhanced the nutrient content, including potassium (184.91 %), calcium (71.65 %), copper (169.71 %), iron (129.8 %), magnesium (94.08 %), manganese (168.78 %), and zinc (102.89 %), phosphorus (129.37 %) and nitrogen (68.68 %) in grains, which were mediated by nutrient-specific transporters. Transcriptome analysis further revealed that BC. NPs significantly upregulated photosynthetic pigments (LOC4327993, LOC4334300, LOC4336028, LOC4338498, LOC4343070) and nutrient-related genes (LOC4333080, LOC4342829, and LOC4347964) under elevated CO2. Comparing E. CK vs E. BC. NPs combined comparative analysis, 27459 genes, 247 exhibiting downregulation, and 319 upregulation. Furthermore, GO terms and KEGG enrichment highlighted nutrient acquisition, the increased activity of amino acid biosynthesis and nitrogen metabolism pathways under combined treatment, further supporting improved nutrient homeostasis. These findings suggest the first evidence that BC amendment and CeO2-NPs synergistically act as nanoelicitors for physiological and transcriptomic alteration in crops and improve the nutrient content under climatic stress.
